colorants.label: Cobalt Co²⁺
colorants.why: Tetrahedral Co²⁺ absorbs across the orange and red — a fraction of a percent gives a deep blue.

colorants.label: Chromium Cr³⁺
colorants.why: Two ligand-field bands — violet-blue and orange-red — leave a green transmission window.

colorants.label: Manganese Mn³⁺
colorants.why: A broad absorption centered in the green leaves purple — the amethyst of decolorized glass gone purple.

colorants.label: Iron Fe²⁺
colorants.why: A strong absorption rising into the red and near-IR gives the blue-green edge tint of ordinary window glass.

colorants.label: Iron Fe³⁺
colorants.why: Weak absorption confined to the violet edge — a pale straw yellow. Same element as Fe²⁺, different color: that's redox.

colorants.label: Copper Cu²⁺
colorants.why: Strong red absorption leaves turquoise — the Egyptian-faience lineage.

colorants.label: Gold colloid
colorants.why: Surface plasmon resonance near 530 nm devours the green — the transmitted remainder is gold-ruby red.

colorants.label: Silver colloid
colorants.why: Silver's plasmon sits near 415 nm, in the blue — transmitting the yellow of silver stain.

colorants.label: CdS crystals
colorants.why: A 2.4 eV band gap absorbs everything bluer than ~515 nm with a razor edge — brilliant yellow.

colorants.label: CdS–Se crystals
colorants.why: Swap sulfur for selenium and the edge slides — the same chemistry tuned to orange.

colorants.label: CdSe-rich crystals
colorants.why: Selenium-rich crystals push the edge to ~640 nm — the saturated signal red of the selenium ruby.

colorants.label: Neodymium Nd³⁺
colorants.why: Shielded 4f electrons absorb in narrow pickets (green, yellow, near-IR) — a lilac that changes with the light source.

recipes.label: Decolorize
recipes.story: Manganese's purple absorbs where iron's green transmits — they cancel toward gray, the old glassmaker's trick. (The real trick also involves redox chemistry this model can't show.)

recipes.label: Deep teal
recipes.story: Cobalt devours the orange and red; chromium devours the violet and red — stack them and only a blue-green window survives.

recipes.label: Amber-ish
recipes.story: Ferric iron's violet edge plus manganese's green dip leave a warm straw-amber. Real beer-bottle amber needs the ferri-sulfide chromophore — this is only the spectral half of the story.
